video_title: Is DePIN Entering Its Next Growth Phase?
gist: Blockworks' Lightspeed panel covers three on-chain themes: tokenized spot equities taking off on Solana (Backpack's redeemable SpaceX token, Micron next), the FOMO social-trading app's $75M Series B at a $550M valuation, and Helium/HNT, where Helium Mobile was sold to Andrew Yang's Noble Mobile and the contentious HIP 149 proposal would mint ~141M HNT over 36 months while retiring proof-of-coverage. Despite a recent video framing of 'DePIN's next growth phase,' the deeper take is that the old front-loaded-emissions DePIN model is 'dead,' with capital-origination (USDAI-style) models like Daylight and Dawn now the frontier.

insights.claim: Backpack's tokenized SpaceX equity drove a large spike in tokenized-equities trading volume on Solana over the week and a half prior to the recording, making SpaceX the dominant single driver of the category's takeoff.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Watch tokenized-equity volume on Solana as a leading indicator of demand for on-chain spot equities; concentration in one name means the trend's durability depends on follow-on listings.

insights.claim: Backpack's tokenized-equity structure is materially superior to X stocks because the token carries an actual claim that can be moved between a brokerage account and on-chain, which lets market makers quote tighter and provide deeper liquidity.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Redeemable one-for-one structures, not SPV wrappers, are the market-structure edge for deep liquidity in tokenized equities; favor venues using that model.
insights.quote: you actually have a claim on the equity in the sense that you can move it between your brokerage account and and your your token

insights.claim: The SpaceX tokenized equity launch risks being a 'Trump coin' top, where the largest IPO in history sets an anchor that subsequent tokenized listings cannot match in volume or returns.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Don't extrapolate SpaceX-level volumes to future listings; fade hype on incremental tokenized names unless a new catalyst drives them.
insights.quote: I don't know if you can see something like, uh, something similar with SpaceX where people are anchoring what they expect tokenized volumes to be

insights.claim: Tokenized-equity volume on Coinbase's Base chain has scaled from essentially nothing a few months ago to a few million dollars per day, mirroring X stocks' earlier trajectory on Solana, and could jump sharply if Coinbase pushes the product into its main app.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Base could become a second venue that adds to rather than cannibalizes Solana volume; a Coinbase app rollout is the catalyst to watch, with COIN as a distant downstream play.

insights.claim: Listing velocity is the key competitive variable for tokenized-equity venues, which must forecast and list whatever asset will be hot a week ahead, replicating Hyperliquid's trend-following playbook (spy, the cues, Nvidia, oil, silver).
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Bet on the venue that lists the next hot equity fastest; OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs are the obvious next velocity test.
insights.quote: I think you have to be positioned for whatever's going to drive the next wave of hype in equity markets

insights.claim: OpenAI and Anthropic have reportedly filed confidential IPO paperwork and could go public before the end of 2026, with total equity issuance expected to set records well beyond 2021 levels.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: An OpenAI or Anthropic IPO would be the next major catalyst for tokenized-equity volume; position venues and on-chain liquidity ahead of those listings.
insights.quote: equity issuance is expected to like go through the roof and set new records like well beyond that of 2021

insights.claim: The pre-IPO SpaceX 'pre-stocks' token trades at roughly a 33% discount to spot mark price, driven primarily by a mandatory post-IPO lockup unlocking in tranches over the first six months plus SPV ownership costs, a dynamic many retail buyers did not realize.
insights.speaker: Joe / Carlos
insights.so_what: Discounts on pre-IPO tokens reflect lockups, not mispricing; treat the discount as a clock on the unlock schedule rather than free alpha.
insights.quote: the pre-stocks variant of SpaceX is currently trading at a 33% discount to kind of like the spot mark price of the stock

insights.claim: Backpack's post-IPO SpaceX tokenized equity trades at roughly a 0.5% premium to market price, versus the 33% discount on the SPV-backed pre-stocks variant, illustrating that redeemable one-for-one structure closes the basis.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Pricing tightness is direct evidence of structure quality; the premium-vs-discount gap is a clean signal of which wrapper to trust.
insights.quote: the SpaceX Backpack uh tokenized equity is trading at like a 0.5% premium to the market price

insights.claim: Anthropic reportedly indicated a month or two prior that it would not honor certain SPVs claiming exposure to the company, adding counterparty risk to pre-IPO tokenized exposure structured through SPVs.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: SPV-wrapped pre-IPO tokens carry issuer-recognition risk; verify the underlying claim is honored before treating these as equivalent to shares.
insights.quote: Anthropic saying it's not going to honor some of these like SPVs that have supposedly have, you know, uh some investment into the company

insights.claim: The 'decentralized Nasdaq' thesis extends beyond equities: Solana is positioned as a permissionless venue to trade any asset class on-chain, including commodities, precious metals, and collectibles, the last of which has notably taken off on Solana.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Frame Solana's TAM as all tradable assets, not just equities; collectibles volume is an underrated demand vector to monitor.
insights.quote: the entire point of like this decentralized Nasdaq vision is that Solana becomes this permissionless venue where you can trade any asset

themes.theme: FOMO and the social-trading app cycle

insights.claim: Social-trading app FOMO raised $75 million at a $550 million valuation in a Series B and is running at roughly a $20M annualized revenue rate on its trailing-30-day daily revenue, implying a ~20-25x sales multiple.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: A 20-25x sales multiple on a meme-dependent trading app frames the risk: justified only if revenue diversifies beyond meme coins.
insights.quote: raised 75 million at a 550 million dollar valuation series B

insights.claim: Solana trading-platform revenue has collapsed from over $100M in weekly revenue in the week after the Trump coin launch, to ~$16M a year ago, to ~$4M last week, showing a structural downtrend tied to declining meme-coin activity.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Trading-frontend revenue is a high-beta proxy for meme-coin speculation; the ~25x decline from peak warns against valuing these apps on peak-cycle revenue.
insights.quote: the trading platforms on Solana made over a hundred million dollars just in weekly revenue

insights.claim: FOMO has grown steadily 'up and to the right' since launch without yet being hit by the cyclical meme-coin downturn that has eroded Axiom and pump.fun, because it built a fresh user base rather than riding the prior meme wave.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: FOMO's counter-cyclical growth is the bull case; the key risk is whether it eventually converges to the Axiom/BonkBot/Photon decline pattern.

insights.claim: Wallet incumbents like Phantom, MetaMask, and Rabby are monetizing legacy self-custody users via in-app swaps, while FOMO targets a net-new, TikTok-like high-engagement trader less anchored to legacy crypto.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: The user-acquisition strategy differentiates FOMO's durability; net-new users could lengthen its runway versus incumbents milking existing bases.

insights.claim: FOMO is multi-chain and supports trading any tokenized asset from day one (SpaceX on Solana, ICOs on Ethereum L2s), giving it a path to revenue diversification beyond meme coins toward equities and perps.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Multi-chain, all-asset coverage is FOMO's hedge against meme-coin decay; total cross-chain revenue and asset-type mix are the metrics to demand.

insights.claim: FOMO's Hyperliquid builder-code (perps) revenue is only a few thousand dollars a day, peaking around $15k, while its on-chain spot trading drives roughly $60k a day, so perps are not yet meaningful to its business.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: FOMO's economics still hinge on spot (largely meme) volume; perps adoption is the upside lever that has not yet materialized.

insights.claim: Each successive generation of crypto trading frontends (Photon, Trojan, BonkBot, Axiom, FOMO) becomes more full-featured year over year, but historically each gets washed out by the next entrant, posing the core durability question for FOMO.
insights.speaker: host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Frontend dominance is historically transient; underwrite FOMO on whether it can break the displacement cycle, not on current growth alone.

insights.claim: Trading is becoming socially embedded, with FOMO weaving X handles and trade-sharing into profiles, and a recurring market pattern where AI/semiconductor analysts drop tickers on Substack and those names move on a weekly basis.
insights.speaker: Carlos / host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Social/copy-trading is a contested land grab (Robinhood, Zora, Twitter cash-tags); whoever nails distribution of tickers-to-followers captures the next flow.

themes.theme: Helium Mobile sale and HNT economics

insights.claim: Helium Mobile was acquired by Noble Mobile, the consumer carrier built by former presidential candidate Andrew Yang, which inherits a five-figure (tens of thousands) active subscriber base out of ~700,000 total sign-ups and will continue routing traffic over the Helium network.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: The sale removes a cost center from Helium Labs while preserving network traffic; the retained routing agreement matters more for HNT than the subscriber transfer.

insights.claim: Helium Mobile (the for-profit carrier under Nova Labs, separate from the DAO-owned network) was driving only a very small share of Helium network traffic, with the majority of activity now coming from AT&T and T-Mobile carrier offload partners.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: HNT's fundamental driver is third-party carrier offload, not Helium's in-house carrier; investor focus should be on AT&T/T-Mobile offload growth.
insights.quote: The majority of the activity today is happening from carrier offload from carrier partners as opposed to Nova's in-house carrier

insights.claim: The Helium Mobile revenue buyback-and-burn pathway for HNT holders, worth roughly $1.5M a month as of May, was closed off in January 2026, eliminating that direct value-accrual channel to the token.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: A ~$1.5M/month buy-and-burn sink is gone, removing a tailwind for HNT supply dynamics that holders previously relied on.
insights.quote: That pathway closed off in January of 2026

insights.claim: Helium daily network revenue fell roughly 80% (from ~$50k/day to ~$10k/day starting early June) not because data-transfer volumes dropped, but because the team cut its per-gigabyte subsidy from 50 cents to a 10-cent floor.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: The revenue drop reflects removal of an unsustainable subsidy, not demand loss; transfer volumes are the real fundamental and remain intact.
insights.quote: the the drop off here is caused by a lack of subsidization

insights.claim: Helium's reported revenue now better reflects the network's true economics after the team stopped subsidizing the per-gigabyte rate to 50 cents (well above market) off the foundation/LabCo balance sheet.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Subsidization had been masking real unit economics; the lower printed revenue is a truer baseline for valuing HNT going forward.

insights.claim: HIP 149 is the most material upcoming change for HNT holders, bundling four decisions: a 10-cent/GB earnings floor, an operations-and-growth supplement, retiring proof-of-coverage, and a community-elected advisory council.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: HIP 149 is the single governance event to model for HNT; its passage reshapes emissions, the earnings floor, and reward allocation.
insights.quote: the HIP 149 proposal on the table is probably the most material change that holders are going to have in the coming months

insights.claim: The most consequential part of HIP 149 mints about 141 million HNT over 36 months into a multi-sig controlled by Nova Labs plus a community-elected council, flat for the first 12 months then decaying over the following 24.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: A front-loaded ~141M HNT mint is dilutive in the near-to-medium term; the market must digest the supplement before any long-term emissions decline benefits holders.
insights.quote: about 141 million HNT being minted over the course of 36 months into a multi-sig controlled by the Nova Labs team as well as a community elected council

insights.claim: HIP 149 would retire proof-of-coverage rewards (currently ~25% of HNT emissions) and reallocate them to data transfer, the network's actual economic driver.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Shifting ~25% of emissions from 'existing' rewards to usage-based data transfer ties token issuance more directly to revenue, a structurally positive realignment.

insights.claim: Helium's new CEO Mario Tio set a 'North Star' of roughly 7-10x current daily traffic as the level where Helium gains pricing leverage over carriers and the flywheel begins to compound.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: 7-10x current traffic is the explicit milestone for HNT to inflect; track daily traffic toward that threshold as the key fundamental KPI.
insights.quote: roughly 7 to 10x current traffic current daily traffic volumes on the network is where Helium begins to have like leverage

insights.claim: Helium's international expansion, especially Brazil and Mexico, offers higher rates than the US because creating net-new coverage commands better economics than supplementing existing coverage, though Telco sales cycles are slow.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Net-new-coverage markets in South America are the higher-margin growth catalyst for Helium; the Brazil rollout is the one already in action to watch.
insights.quote: if you can create net new coverage as opposed to supplementing existing coverage, the rates happen to be much higher

insights.claim: On non-revenue metrics, every Helium network-level chart (data transfer and daily active users) has been 'up and to the right' for over a year as more carriers adopt offload and existing carriers route more traffic.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Usage fundamentals decouple from the subsidy-driven revenue drop; rising data transfer and DAUs are the bullish signal under the noisy revenue line.
insights.quote: everything is up into the right and has been for over a year

insights.claim: The traditional DePIN model of front-loading token emissions to bootstrap a physical network is effectively dead, with nearly every new DePIN launch over the past year performing poorly and the market disillusioned with it.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Avoid new DePIN tokens that rely on emissions-bootstrapping; the bootstrapping mechanism is now the single most consequential founder decision and a key screen.
insights.quote: I think the old model is is dead. I'm not seeing anyone really trying to leverage that model anymore

insights.claim: Daylight and Dawn are building DePIN networks using a USDAI-style capital-origination model, incentivizing capital onto chain structured like loans rather than front-loading emissions, letting them fund and time their own installations.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Capital-origination DePIN is the emerging frontier; Daylight and Dawn are the names to track as the post-emissions model template.

insights.claim: USDAI saw significant outflows after its points program ended, but its yields have not decreased and sit roughly halfway to terminal yield (full allocation, nothing in T-bills), indicating a sustainable yield source.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: Points-program outflows are not the same as yield collapse; USDAI's maintained yield validates the capital-origination model's sustainability claim.

insights.claim: Capital-origination DePIN models risk locking depositor capital for the lifespan of the physical asset (e.g., Daylight's solar panels run 15-25 years), so airdrop-farming capital could get stuck in multi-year redemption queues.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter / host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Duration mismatch is the core risk for capital committing to these models; weigh whether a few points of excess yield justifies 15-25 year illiquidity.
insights.quote: a solar panel is 15 to 25 years in terms of lifespan. Like I wouldn't necessarily want to have my capital locked for 25 years because I'm stuck in the queue

insights.claim: GeoNet is the DePIN outlier success: now the largest RTK network in the world with the most coverage and best pricing, having eclipsed its two larger competitors, proving upfront emissions-bootstrapping can still scale in a high-switching-cost niche.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter
insights.so_what: GeoNet is the counterexample to the 'old model is dead' thesis; high switching costs plus real coverage leadership make it a standout DePIN name.

insights.claim: GeoNet's tokenomics are far simpler than almost any other DePIN project, and across crypto more broadly, the more complex tokenomic models (two- and three-token systems, staking conversions) have tended to produce poorer holder outcomes.
insights.speaker: Nick Carter / host (Danny)
insights.so_what: Use tokenomic simplicity as a positive screen; concise, easy-to-explain token models (and transparency frameworks) correlate with better holder outcomes.
insights.quote: the the more complex tokenomic models tended to lead to poorer outcomes for holders
